title: Drone ban lifted at Coogee Beach after woman mauled by shark
text:
Advertisement

A ban on drones will be temporarily lifted at Coogee Beach to allow Surf Life Saving NSW to scan for sharks following a terrifying attack that left a woman fighting for her life in hospital. The Civil Aviation Safety Authority (CASA) bans drones from being flown at Coogee because the beach is in the flight path of commercial airlines, but granted a temporary exemption on Sunday in response to the attack. Coogee Beach remains closed after a shark attack left a female swimmer in a critical condition in Sydney. Janie Barrett Minister for Agriculture Tara Moriarty said the government would work to make the exemption permanent, but would not answer questions as to why an exemption hadn’t previously been sought. The ban was in place “for good reason”, she said. “We’re working with Surf Life Saving New South Wales to make sure that we can embrace this technology above our beaches,” she said, adding the government was funding Surf Life Saving NSW to train staff to pilot drones and to purchase two new drones.

Advertisement

Surf Life Saving NSW chief executive Steve Pearce said the organisation would push for the ban on flying drones over Coogee Beach to be permanently lifted. “Drones have proven to be the number one factor in the shark mitigation strategy for swimmer and surfer protection,” Pearce said. Related Article Shark ‘I couldn’t fight off the shark’: The hero paddleboarder who rescued Coogee attack victim Surf Life Saving NSW drones have detected more than 2000 sharks along the state’s coastline since last July, Pearce said, and allowed more than 1000 interventions to protect swimmers and surfers from sharks. The attack on Saturday left a 35-year-old woman in a critical condition, and followed a series of similar incidents over summer which increased pressure on the Minns government over its shark mitigation strategies. The Treasurer Daniel Mookhey said on Sunday that the government was already funding the “world’s biggest” shark mitigation strategy but would “obviously look to see whether or not any additional money is necessary” ahead of the state budget next week.

Advertisement

Moriarty said she had sought urgent advice from the Department of Primary Industries (DPI) and Regional Development, which runs the shark mitigation programme. While she would not rule out a potential shark cull, Moriarty stressed the focus would be on technology, including unmanned AI drones. Surf Life Saving NSW drones have detected more than 2000 sharks since last July. “The priority for keeping people safe is more drones in the sky, tracking and tagging more sharks so that we can understand their movements, and people can make decisions – informed decisions and educated decisions – about swimming at our beaches,” she said. “We want to make sure that it can be available on every beach.”

Advertisement

White sharks would not be targeted in the ban due to their protected status. Aviation expert Neil Hansford said he couldn’t see why the exemption couldn’t be made permanent, so long as drones were operated by qualified pilots through Surf Life Saving NSW or emergency services. “You can’t use drones at Coogee because it’s under the flight path onto the short runway at Mascot. This sort of ban is not uncommon,” he said. “[But] if they accepted a fixed rule not to fly them above, say 500 feet or 1000 feet, and limited them to that, I think it would be easily negotiated. A thousand feet would be very safe.” In a statement, a spokesman for CASA said while current approvals only allowed the use of drones at Coogee in an emergency due to its proximity to Sydney Airport, it would negotiate with Surf Life Saving NSW “to explore whether the current approvals for operating at Coogee can be safely adapted to better support their operations”.

Advertisement

The shark attack shocked a number of experts, including DPI shark program lead Marcel Green. “[It] was quite a unique and different incident,” he said, adding the swimmer was “doing everything right” by swimming during the day, between the flags and in crystal clear water. Related Article Analysis

Sharks Shark bites are becoming more common – but a cull won’t work “It was just one of those random events. We can never be protected 100 per cent at every beach, every day, all the time,” he said. The 35-year-old woman was in a critical but stable condition at St Vincent’s Hospital on Sunday morning. Health Minister Ryan Park said he had spoken to one of the emergency physicians who had provided first aid to the woman after the attack who described it as “a harrowing scene”.

Advertisement

“[T]his was a scene that was graphic in detail. She was obviously transported, having lost an enormous amount of blood, into being in a critical condition,” he said. While the woman was in a stable condition, Park said she was “far from out of the woods”. Moriarty and Member for Coogee Marjorie O’Neill thanked the first responders and said their thoughts were with the victim, a 35-year-old woman. She is in critical condition in hospital. “We know many people in our community are scared, frightened, afraid to get back in the water,” she said. A community event will be hosted at Coogee Surf Life Saving Club on Monday evening to assist those seeking advice and support. Be the first to know when major news happens. title: Knicks snap 53-year NBA drought
text:
Knicks snap 53-year NBA drought

After more than half a century, the New York Knicks clinch an NBA championship over the San Antonio Spurs.

title: New York Knicks break drought to win NBA championship
text:
Advertisement

Breaking SportBasketballNBA New York Knicks break drought to win NBA championship Updated June 14, 2026 — 1:44pm ,first published 1:38pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Share A A A

San Antonio: Jalen Brunson and the Comeback Knicks did it again. And now they’re the Champion Knicks. For the first time in 53 years, New York rules the NBA. Brunson scored 45 points, including 13 straight for New York in the fourth quarter, and the Knicks beat the San Antonio Spurs 94-90 in game five of the NBA finals on Saturday night (Sunday AEST). Loading The Knicks won the series 4-1, rallying from double-digit deficits in all four of those victories. The deficit was 16 in the decisive match. Brunson and the Knicks were never fazed. Brunson, fittingly, closed with a flourish. He set a Knicks record for points in a finals game; it had been 38 by Willis Reed against the Los Angeles Lakers in Game 3 of the 1970 series. It now belongs to the left-handed point guard who changed the franchise’s fortunes when he arrived four years ago.

Advertisement

Mikal Bridges and Josh Hart – the other two parts of the “Nova Knicks” trio that also includes Brunson, three players who were NCAA champions at Villanova and teamed up in New York to try to do the same – combined to score 27 points. Bridges had 14, Hart 13. Brunson won NCAA crowns twice with Villanova – both in Texas, the 2016 one in Houston and the 2018 one in San Antonio, just a few miles away from the arena that the Spurs call home. New York Knicks center Karl-Anthony Towns, right, hugs forward Og Anunoby during the after defeating the San Antonio Spurs to win the NBA title. AP Photo/Darren Abate A Texas three-step of titles, and this one was surely the sweetest of all. Actor Sydney Sweeney and businessman Scooter Braun were among the high-profile fans in the crowd for the deciding match.

Advertisement

Dylan Harper scored 25 for the Spurs, who got 19 points, 14 rebounds and five blocked shots from Victor Wembanyama. The Knicks improved to 4-0 in closeout opportunities this season, winning them all on the road. It didn’t feel like the road, though – not with thousands of New York faithful having made the trip to Texas to see a moment 53 years in the making. View post on X New York got to the brink of this title by rallying from 29 points down in game four to win 107-106 on OG Anunoby’s tip-in with 1.2 seconds left on Thursday (AEST). It was the largest comeback in NBA finals history and the biggest comeback in any game this season, regular season or play-offs. By comparison, then, a 16-point rally in this one seemed easy.

title: Huge move after Sydney shark attack
text:
Nothing is off the table in terms of tackling sharks on NSW beaches the state’s Minister for Agriculture Tara Moriarty said, as a woman remains in a critical condition following a shark attack in broad daylight at Coogee beach.

An exemption to a ban on drone flight has been granted by authorities in the wake of the attack, with hopes it could be made permanent.

“Nothing is off the table in terms of how we keep the community safe,” she said.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

“But we swim in the ocean, with living, breathing animals, and we have to share the ocean every day.”

Ms Moriarty said they would be prioritising technology, such as the drones the NSW government has invested in for all beaches across the state, however they were not deployed at Coogee because of the proximity to the airport.

“Surf Life Saving New South Wales have applied to CASA for an urgent … exemption to be able to fly drones over Coogee Beach, that has been granted, and we, as a New South Wales government, will work with Surf Life Saving New South Wales to work with CASA to make sure that that becomes permanent,” she said.

Drones were not deployed at Coogee because of the proximity to the airport. Picture: Instagram

NSW Minister for Agriculture, Tara Moriarty said nothing is off the table. Picture: NewsWire / Damian Shaw

Ms Moriarty thanked the first responders who helped the woman after the attack. Picture: NewsWire / Damian Shaw

Ms Moriarty said it was a “very devastating day” at Coogee beach and thanked everyone who was there to help the woman.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

“We’ve seen too many of them over the course of this summer, and my heart goes out to the young woman who is still in hospital and to her family,” she said.

“We wish her a speedy, full recovery.

“I also want to thank everybody who was here yesterday … It’s a very traumatic experience for the people who were involved in that.”

Tony Abbott has joined calls for shark culling on NSW beaches.

“I just read the latest news about a woman fighting for her life after a shark attack at Coogee beach,” he said in a video posted to social media

“It’s so wrong that we don’t cull sharks after attacks, it’s so wrong that we don’t have a commercial shark fishery given the explosion in shark numbers and it’s so wrong that we don’t put people before sharks.”

Tony Abbott has joined calls for shark culling on NSW beaches. Picture: Instagram

An off-duty lifeguard was in the water when the woman was attacked and dragged her to shore. Picture: NewsWire / Damian Shaw

Off-duty lifeguard Charlie Verco was in the water when the woman was attacked and dragged her to shore on his board.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

“I was paddling through Coogee and just about 100m ahead of me, a group of swimmers started shouting shark,” he told ABC Weekend Breakfast.

“Most of the time it’s usually not a shark.

“But as I started paddling over close towards them, I could see this thing underwater moving quite slowly and I almost thought it was moving inquisitively and I thought it would just have a look at them, realise they weren’t food and leave them alone.”

Mr Verco is in the water most days and said he has seen an increase in sharks over the last few years.

“I’d say there’d be more in the last year than I’ve heard or seen in my entire life before that,” he said.

Advertisement Advertisement

Advertisement Advertisement

“I personally don’t feel as safe going to the water now.”

The woman was doing all the right things, swimming close to shore in the middle of the day and between the flags. Picture: NewsWire / Damian Shaw

Mr Verco said the woman was doing all the right things, swimming close to shore on one of the busiest beaches on a really clear day, in the middle of the day and between the flags.

“It just shows that you can be vulnerable at any point now,” he said.

Coogee MP Marjorie O’Neill said a community event will be held at the Coogee Surf Club on Monday.

“For those in our community who would like some advice, they don’t know how to talk to their kids about getting back into the water,” she said.

“We want to do everything that we can to keep our beaches safe.”
